Crime — Definition.rule: A crime requires a voluntary act or omission accompanied by a culpable mental state.
Crime — Definition.application: Because the defendant committed a voluntary act with the required mental state, a crime exists.

Actus Reus — Voluntary Act.rule: Actus reus requires a voluntary physical act by the defendant.
Actus Reus — Voluntary Act.application: Because the act was voluntary, actus reus is satisfied.

Actus Reus — Omission.rule: An omission may satisfy actus reus only when the defendant had a legal duty to act and failed to do so.
Actus Reus — Omission.application: Because the defendant had a legal duty and failed to act, actus reus is satisfied.

Legal Duties to Act.rule: Legal duties to act arise from statute, contract, special relationship, voluntary assumption of care, or creation of the peril.
Legal Duties to Act.application: Because a special relationship existed, a legal duty arose.

Mens Rea — General Principle.rule: Mens rea is the culpable mental state required to commit a crime.
Mens Rea — General Principle.application: Because the defendant possessed the required mental state, mens rea is satisfied.

Common Law Mens Rea Categories.rule: Common law mens rea categories include intent, knowledge, recklessness, and negligence.
Common Law Mens Rea Categories.application: Because the defendant acted knowingly, the mens rea requirement is met.

Intent — Specific Intent.rule: Specific intent crimes require that the defendant intended to bring about a particular result.
Intent — Specific Intent.application: Because the defendant intended the result, specific intent is established.

Intent — General Intent.rule: General intent crimes require only that the defendant intended to perform the prohibited act.
Intent — General Intent.application: Because the defendant intended the act, general intent is satisfied.

Transferred Intent.rule: Transferred intent applies when a defendant intends to harm one victim but accidentally harms another.
Transferred Intent.application: Because intent transfers, liability applies to the actual victim.

Concurrence.rule: The actus reus and mens rea must coincide in time for criminal liability.
Concurrence.application: Because intent existed at the time of the act, concurrence is satisfied.

Strict Liability Crimes.rule: Strict liability crimes require no mens rea and typically involve public welfare offenses.
Strict Liability Crimes.application: Because the offense is strict liability, intent is irrelevant.

Homicide — Definition.rule: Homicide is the unlawful killing of another human being.
Homicide — Definition.application: Because the killing was unlawful, homicide occurred.

Murder — Common Law Definition.rule: Common law murder is the unlawful killing of another human being with malice aforethought.
Murder — Common Law Definition.application: Because the defendant acted with malice aforethought, murder is established.

Malice Aforethought — Intent to Kill.rule: Malice aforethought exists when the defendant intends to kill another human being.
Malice Aforethought — Intent to Kill.application: Because the defendant intended to kill, malice aforethought is present.

Malice Aforethought — Intent to Cause Se.rule: Malice aforethought exists when the defendant intends to cause serious bodily injury and death results.
Malice Aforethought — Intent to Cause Se.application: Because the defendant intended serious bodily injury, malice is satisfied.

Malice Aforethought — Depraved Heart.rule: Malice aforethought exists when the defendant acts with reckless indifference to an unjustifiably high risk to human life.
Malice Aforethought — Depraved Heart.application: Because the conduct showed extreme recklessness, malice aforethought exists.

Malice Aforethought — Felony Murder.rule: Malice aforethought is implied when a death occurs during the commission or attempted commission of a felony.
Malice Aforethought — Felony Murder.application: Because the death occurred during a felony, felony murder applies.

Felony Murder — Inherently Dangerous Fel.rule: Felony murder applies only when the underlying felony is inherently dangerous to human life.
Felony Murder — Inherently Dangerous Fel.application: Because the felony was inherently dangerous, felony murder applies.

Felony Murder — Merger Doctrine.rule: The felony murder doctrine does not apply when the underlying felony merges with the homicide.
Felony Murder — Merger Doctrine.application: Because the felony merged, felony murder is unavailable.

Felony Murder — Agency Rule.rule: Under the agency rule, felony murder liability applies only when the killing is committed by the defendant or a co-felon.
Felony Murder — Agency Rule.application: Because the killing was committed by a co-felon, liability applies.

Felony Murder — Proximate Cause Rule.rule: Under the proximate cause rule, felony murder liability applies if the death is a foreseeable result of the felony.
Felony Murder — Proximate Cause Rule.application: Because the death was foreseeable, felony murder applies.

Degrees of Murder — First Degree.rule: First-degree murder requires willful, deliberate, and premeditated killing or murder committed during enumerated felonies.
Degrees of Murder — First Degree.application: Because the killing was premeditated, first-degree murder applies.

Degrees of Murder — Second Degree.rule: Second-degree murder includes all other murders that do not qualify as first-degree murder.
Degrees of Murder — Second Degree.application: Because the murder lacked premeditation, it is second-degree.

Voluntary Manslaughter.rule: Voluntary manslaughter is an intentional killing mitigated by adequate provocation or heat of passion.
Voluntary Manslaughter.application: Because the defendant acted in the heat of passion, the charge is mitigated.

Adequate Provocation.rule: Adequate provocation exists when a reasonable person would lose self-control under the circumstances.
Adequate Provocation.application: Because a reasonable person would lose control, provocation is adequate.

Involuntary Manslaughter — Criminal Negl.rule: Involuntary manslaughter occurs when a death results from criminal negligence.
Involuntary Manslaughter — Criminal Negl.application: Because the defendant acted negligently, involuntary manslaughter applies.

Involuntary Manslaughter — Misdemeanor M.rule: Involuntary manslaughter occurs when a death results from the commission of a non-dangerous misdemeanor.
Involuntary Manslaughter — Misdemeanor M.application: Because death resulted from a misdemeanor, involuntary manslaughter applies.

Vehicular Manslaughter.rule: Vehicular manslaughter occurs when a death results from negligent or reckless operation of a motor vehicle.
Vehicular Manslaughter.application: Because the defendant drove recklessly, vehicular manslaughter applies.

Inchoate Crimes — Definition.rule: Inchoate crimes punish conduct undertaken toward the commission of another crime even if the crime is not completed.
Inchoate Crimes — Definition.application: Because the defendant took steps toward a crime, inchoate liability applies.

Attempt — Definition.rule: Attempt requires the specific intent to commit a crime and a substantial step toward its commission.
Attempt — Definition.application: Because the defendant intended the crime and took a substantial step, attempt is established.

Attempt — Substantial Step.rule: A substantial step is conduct that strongly corroborates the defendant’s criminal purpose.
Attempt — Substantial Step.application: Because the conduct corroborated intent, the substantial step requirement is satisfied.

Attempt — Impossibility.rule: Factual impossibility is not a defense to attempt, while legal impossibility is a defense.
Attempt — Impossibility.application: Because the impossibility was factual, attempt liability remains.

Solicitation — Definition.rule: Solicitation occurs when a defendant intentionally invites, requests, or encourages another to commit a crime.
Solicitation — Definition.application: Because the defendant encouraged another to commit a crime, solicitation is established.

Solicitation — Completion.rule: Solicitation is complete once the request or encouragement is made, regardless of whether the crime is committed.
Solicitation — Completion.application: Because the request was made, solicitation is complete.

Conspiracy — Definition.rule: Conspiracy requires an agreement between two or more persons to commit a crime and intent to enter the agreement.
Conspiracy — Definition.application: Because the parties agreed to commit a crime, conspiracy exists.

Conspiracy — Overt Act.rule: Most jurisdictions require an overt act in furtherance of the conspiracy by any conspirator.
Conspiracy — Overt Act.application: Because an overt act occurred, the conspiracy is complete.

Conspiracy — Pinkerton Liability.rule: Under Pinkerton liability, a conspirator is liable for crimes committed by co-conspirators in furtherance of the conspiracy and that are foreseeable.
Conspiracy — Pinkerton Liability.application: Because the crime was foreseeable and in furtherance, liability attaches.

Conspiracy — Withdrawal.rule: Withdrawal from a conspiracy requires an affirmative act that communicates abandonment to co-conspirators.
Conspiracy — Withdrawal.application: Because the defendant communicated withdrawal, future liability is cut off.

Merger — Attempt and Solicitation.rule: Attempt and solicitation merge into the completed crime if the crime is successfully committed.
Merger — Attempt and Solicitation.application: Because the crime was completed, attempt merges into the offense.

Merger — Conspiracy.rule: Conspiracy does not merge into the completed crime.
Merger — Conspiracy.application: Because conspiracy is separate, liability remains.

Accomplice Liability — Definition.rule: An accomplice is one who intentionally aids, abets, or encourages the commission of a crime.
Accomplice Liability — Definition.application: Because the defendant intentionally assisted the crime, accomplice liability applies.

Accomplice Liability — Mens Rea.rule: Accomplice liability requires the intent to assist the principal in committing the crime.
Accomplice Liability — Mens Rea.application: Because the defendant intended to assist, the mens rea requirement is satisfied.

Accomplice Liability — Act Requirement.rule: Accomplice liability requires some act of aiding, abetting, counseling, or encouraging the principal.
Accomplice Liability — Act Requirement.application: Because the defendant provided assistance, the act requirement is met.

Mere Presence.rule: Mere presence at the scene of a crime or mere knowledge of the crime is insufficient to establish accomplice liability.
Mere Presence.application: Because the defendant merely observed, accomplice liability does not apply.

Principal — Definition.rule: A principal is the person who actually commits the criminal act.
Principal — Definition.application: Because the defendant committed the act, the defendant is a principal.

Accomplice Liability — Scope of Liabilit.rule: An accomplice is liable for the crimes the accomplice intended to facilitate and any other crimes that are the natural and probable consequences of the criminal conduct.
Accomplice Liability — Scope of Liabilit.application: Because the additional crime was a natural consequence, liability extends.

Accomplice Liability — Natural and Proba.rule: An accomplice is liable for additional crimes that are reasonably foreseeable results of the target crime.
Accomplice Liability — Natural and Proba.application: Because the offense was foreseeable, accomplice liability applies.

Accomplice Liability — Withdrawal.rule: Withdrawal from accomplice liability requires timely notice to the principal and efforts to neutralize the assistance provided.
Accomplice Liability — Withdrawal.application: Because the defendant withdrew and attempted to stop the crime, liability is cut off.

Accomplice Liability — Effect of Princip.rule: An accomplice may be convicted even if the principal is not convicted or is acquitted.
Accomplice Liability — Effect of Princip.application: Because accomplice liability is independent, conviction is permitted.

Battery.rule: Battery is the unlawful application of force to another person resulting in harmful or offensive contact.
Battery.application: Because the defendant caused offensive contact, battery is established.

Aggravated Battery.rule: Aggravated battery occurs when a battery results in serious bodily injury or is committed using a deadly weapon.
Aggravated Battery.application: Because a deadly weapon was used, the battery is aggravated.

Assault — Attempted Battery.rule: Assault occurs when a defendant attempts to commit a battery but fails.
Assault — Attempted Battery.application: Because the defendant attempted but failed to strike the victim, assault applies.

Assault — Reasonable Apprehension.rule: Assault occurs when a defendant intentionally places another in reasonable apprehension of imminent harmful or offensive contact.
Assault — Reasonable Apprehension.application: Because the victim reasonably feared imminent harm, assault is established.

False Imprisonment.rule: False imprisonment is the unlawful confinement of a person without consent or legal authority.
False Imprisonment.application: Because the victim was confined without consent, false imprisonment applies.

Kidnapping.rule: Kidnapping is the unlawful confinement of a person involving movement or concealment that increases the risk of harm.
Kidnapping.application: Because the victim was moved to a secluded location, kidnapping applies.

Rape.rule: Rape is unlawful sexual intercourse accomplished by force or without consent.
Rape.application: Because intercourse occurred without consent, rape is established.

Statutory Rape.rule: Statutory rape is sexual intercourse with a person under the age of consent regardless of consent or mistake of age.
Statutory Rape.application: Because the victim was under the age of consent, statutory rape applies.

Mayhem.rule: Mayhem occurs when a defendant intentionally maims or disfigures another person.
Mayhem.application: Because the defendant permanently disfigured the victim, mayhem applies.

Robbery.rule: Robbery is the taking of personal property from another by force or intimidation with intent to permanently deprive.
Robbery.application: Because force was used during the taking, robbery applies.

Extortion.rule: Extortion is obtaining property through threats of future harm or exposure.
Extortion.application: Because the defendant threatened future harm to obtain property, extortion applies.

Larceny.rule: Larceny is the trespassory taking and carrying away of the personal property of another with intent to permanently deprive.
Larceny.application: Because the defendant trespassorily took and carried away property with intent to permanently deprive, larceny is established.

Larceny — Continuing Trespass.rule: Larceny may be established by continuing trespass when the defendant initially takes property without intent but later forms intent to permanently deprive.
Larceny — Continuing Trespass.application: Because intent formed during possession, continuing trespass applies.

Larceny by Trick.rule: Larceny by trick occurs when possession is obtained through fraud with intent to permanently deprive.
Larceny by Trick.application: Because possession was obtained by fraud, larceny by trick applies.

Embezzlement.rule: Embezzlement is the fraudulent conversion of personal property by a person in lawful possession of the property.
Embezzlement.application: Because the defendant lawfully possessed and converted the property, embezzlement applies.

False Pretenses.rule: False pretenses occurs when title to property is obtained through a false representation of a material fact with intent to defraud.
False Pretenses.application: Because title was transferred by misrepresentation, false pretenses applies.

Robbery — Property Aspect.rule: Robbery is larceny accomplished by force or intimidation from the person or presence of another.
Robbery — Property Aspect.application: Because force accompanied the taking, robbery applies.

Burglary.rule: Burglary is the breaking and entering of a dwelling of another at night with intent to commit a felony inside.
Burglary.application: Because the defendant broke into a dwelling at night intending a felony, burglary applies.

Burglary — Modern Statutes.rule: Modern burglary statutes eliminate the nighttime or dwelling requirements and require unlawful entry with intent to commit a crime.
Burglary — Modern Statutes.application: Because unlawful entry occurred with criminal intent, burglary applies under modern law.

Arson.rule: Arson is the malicious burning of the dwelling of another.
Arson.application: Because the defendant intentionally burned a dwelling, arson applies.

Arson — Modern Statutes.rule: Modern arson statutes include burning of structures or personal property and may require only damage by fire or explosion.
Arson — Modern Statutes.application: Because property was damaged by fire, modern arson applies.

Receiving Stolen Property.rule: Receiving stolen property occurs when a defendant knowingly receives stolen property with intent to permanently deprive the owner.
Receiving Stolen Property.application: Because the defendant knew the property was stolen, liability applies.

Forgery.rule: Forgery is the making or altering of a writing with intent to defraud so that it purports to be the act of another.
Forgery.application: Because the document was altered to deceive, forgery applies.

Uttering a Forged Instrument.rule: Uttering occurs when a defendant knowingly uses or presents a forged instrument with intent to defraud.
Uttering a Forged Instrument.application: Because the forged document was presented knowingly, uttering applies.

Criminal Mischief.rule: Criminal mischief is the intentional or reckless damage or destruction of the property of another.
Criminal Mischief.application: Because the defendant damaged property recklessly, criminal mischief applies.

Insanity — M'Naghten Rule.rule: Under the M'Naghten rule, a defendant is not criminally responsible if, due to mental disease or defect, the defendant did not know the nature of the act or that the act was wrong.
Insanity — M'Naghten Rule.application: Because the defendant did not understand the wrongfulness of the act, the insanity defense applies.

Insanity — Irresistible Impulse Test.rule: Under the irresistible impulse test, a defendant is not responsible if the defendant lacked the capacity to control conduct due to mental disease or defect.
Insanity — Irresistible Impulse Test.application: Because the defendant could not control behavior, the defense applies.

Insanity — Model Penal Code Test.rule: Under the Model Penal Code, a defendant is not responsible if, due to mental disease or defect, the defendant lacked substantial capacity to appreciate the criminality or conform conduct to the law.
Insanity — Model Penal Code Test.application: Because the defendant lacked substantial capacity, the defense applies.

Insanity — Burden of Proof.rule: The defendant generally bears the burden of proving insanity by a preponderance of the evidence.
Insanity — Burden of Proof.application: Because the defendant failed to meet the burden, the defense fails.

Intoxication — Voluntary.rule: Voluntary intoxication is a defense to specific intent crimes but not to general intent crimes.
Intoxication — Voluntary.application: Because the crime required specific intent, voluntary intoxication may negate intent.

Intoxication — Involuntary.rule: Involuntary intoxication is a complete defense if it negates the required mens rea.
Intoxication — Involuntary.application: Because intoxication was involuntary and negated intent, the defense applies.

Self-Defense — General Rule.rule: Self-defense justifies the use of force when the defendant reasonably believes force is necessary to prevent imminent unlawful harm.
Self-Defense — General Rule.application: Because the defendant reasonably feared imminent harm, self-defense applies.

Self-Defense — Deadly Force.rule: Deadly force may be used only to prevent imminent death, serious bodily injury, or forcible sexual assault.
Self-Defense — Deadly Force.application: Because deadly force was necessary to prevent death, the defense applies.

Self-Defense — Duty to Retreat.rule: A defendant must retreat before using deadly force if retreat can be accomplished safely, unless in the defendant’s home.
Self-Defense — Duty to Retreat.application: Because retreat was not required in the home, deadly force was justified.

Defense of Others.rule: A defendant may use force to defend others if the defendant reasonably believes the other person would have the right to self-defense.
Defense of Others.application: Because the third party could defend themselves, the defense applies.

Defense of Property.rule: A defendant may use reasonable non-deadly force to protect property but may not use deadly force solely to protect property.
Defense of Property.application: Because only reasonable force was used, the defense applies.

Necessity.rule: Necessity is a defense when the defendant reasonably believes criminal conduct is necessary to prevent a greater harm.
Necessity.application: Because the harm avoided outweighed the harm caused, necessity applies.

Duress.rule: Duress is a defense when the defendant commits a crime due to an immediate threat of death or serious bodily injury.
Duress.application: Because the defendant acted under immediate threat, duress applies.

Duress — Limitation.rule: Duress is not a defense to intentional homicide.
Duress — Limitation.application: Because the crime was homicide, duress is unavailable.

Mistake of Fact.rule: Mistake of fact is a defense if it negates the required mens rea of the offense.
Mistake of Fact.application: Because the mistake negated intent, the defense applies.

Mistake of Law.rule: Mistake of law is generally not a defense unless the defendant reasonably relied on an official statement of the law.
Mistake of Law.application: Because the defendant relied on official advice, the defense applies.

Entrapment.rule: Entrapment occurs when law enforcement induces a defendant to commit a crime that the defendant was not predisposed to commit.
Entrapment.application: Because the defendant lacked predisposition, entrapment applies.

Fourth Amendment — Scope.rule: The Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable searches and seizures by the government.
Fourth Amendment — Scope.application: Because government conduct was involved, the Fourth Amendment applies.

Government Action Requirement.rule: The Fourth Amendment applies only to government action and not to searches conducted by private individuals acting independently.
Government Action Requirement.application: Because the search was conducted by a private individual, the Fourth Amendment does not apply.

Search — Definition.rule: A search occurs when the government violates a person’s reasonable expectation of privacy.
Search — Definition.application: Because the defendant had a reasonable expectation of privacy, a search occurred.

Reasonable Expectation of Privacy.rule: A reasonable expectation of privacy exists when a person has a subjective expectation of privacy that society recognizes as reasonable.
Reasonable Expectation of Privacy.application: Because society recognizes the expectation as reasonable, the Fourth Amendment applies.

No Search — Open Fields.rule: There is no reasonable expectation of privacy in open fields outside the curtilage of a home.
No Search — Open Fields.application: Because the area was an open field, no search occurred.

Curtilage.rule: Curtilage is the area immediately surrounding a home that is associated with the intimate activities of the home.
Curtilage.application: Because the area was closely connected to the home, it is curtilage.

Search — Physical Intrusion.rule: A search occurs when the government physically intrudes into a constitutionally protected area for the purpose of obtaining information.
Search — Physical Intrusion.application: Because officers physically intruded to gather information, a search occurred.

Seizure of Person.rule: A person is seized when, under the totality of circumstances, a reasonable person would not feel free to leave.
Seizure of Person.application: Because a reasonable person would not feel free to leave, a seizure occurred.

Seizure — Show of Authority.rule: A seizure by show of authority occurs only if the defendant submits to the authority.
Seizure — Show of Authority.application: Because the defendant did not submit, no seizure occurred.

Seizure of Property.rule: Property is seized when the government meaningfully interferes with an individual’s possessory interest.
Seizure of Property.application: Because officers interfered with possession, a seizure occurred.

Warrant — General Rule.rule: A valid warrant must be issued by a neutral and detached magistrate upon probable cause and must particularly describe the place to be searched and the items to be seized.
Warrant — General Rule.application: Because the warrant was properly issued and particularized, it is valid.

Probable Cause — Definition.rule: Probable cause exists when there is a fair probability that evidence of a crime will be found in a particular place.
Probable Cause — Definition.application: Because a fair probability existed, probable cause was established.

Probable Cause — Totality of the Circums.rule: Probable cause is determined under the totality of the circumstances.
Probable Cause — Totality of the Circums.application: Because all circumstances supported a finding of probability, probable cause exists.

Warrant — Particularity Requirement.rule: A warrant must particularly describe the place to be searched and the persons or things to be seized.
buzzwords: persons or things to be seized
Warrant — Particularity Requirement.application: Because the warrant specified the location and items, it satisfies particularity.

Warrant — Execution.rule: A warrant must be executed reasonably and within the scope authorized by the warrant.
Warrant — Execution.application: Because officers stayed within the warrant’s scope, execution was proper.

Knock-and-Announce Rule.rule: Police generally must knock and announce their presence before executing a warrant unless an exception applies.
Knock-and-Announce Rule.application: Because officers complied with the rule, execution was lawful.

Knock-and-Announce — Exceptions.rule: The knock-and-announce requirement is excused when officers reasonably believe it would be dangerous, futile, or result in destruction of evidence.
Knock-and-Announce — Exceptions.application: Because officers feared evidence destruction, the exception applied.

Search Incident to Arrest — Probable Cau.rule: An arrest is valid if supported by probable cause that the suspect committed a crime.
Search Incident to Arrest — Probable Cau.application: Because officers had probable cause, the arrest was valid.

Arrest Warrant — Home Entry.rule: Police may enter a suspect’s home to execute an arrest warrant if there is reason to believe the suspect is inside.
Arrest Warrant — Home Entry.application: Because officers reasonably believed the suspect was inside, entry was permitted.

Search Warrant — Third Party Home.rule: Police may not enter a third party’s home to execute an arrest warrant without a search warrant or consent.
Search Warrant — Third Party Home.application: Because no search warrant was obtained, entry was unlawful.

Good Faith. Exception.rule: Evidence obtained under a defective warrant is admissible if police acted in objectively reasonable reliance on the warrant.
Good Faith. Exception.application: Because officers relied in good faith, the evidence is admissible.

Good Faith — Limitations.rule: The good faith exception does not apply when the magistrate is biased, the warrant is facially deficient, or officers misled the magistrate.
Good Faith — Limitations.application: Because the warrant was facially deficient, good faith does not apply.

Warrant Exceptions — General Rule.rule: A warrantless search is presumptively unreasonable unless it falls within a recognized exception to the warrant requirement.
Warrant Exceptions — General Rule.application: Because the search falls within an exception, no warrant was required.

Consent Search.rule: Police may conduct a warrantless search if a person with actual or apparent authority voluntarily consents.
Consent Search.application: Because consent was voluntarily given by someone with authority, the search was valid.

Consent — Scope.rule: A consent search is limited to the scope of the consent given.
Consent — Scope.application: Because officers stayed within the scope, the search was lawful.

Search Incident to Lawful Arrest — Perso.rule: Police may search the arrestee’s person incident to a lawful arrest without a warrant.
Search Incident to Lawful Arrest — Perso.application: Because the arrest was lawful, the search of the person was valid.

Search Incident to Lawful Arrest — Area.rule: Police may search the area within the arrestee’s immediate control to prevent access to weapons or destruction of evidence.
Search Incident to Lawful Arrest — Area.application: Because the area was within immediate control, the search was valid.

Search Incident to Arrest — Vehicle.rule: Police may search a vehicle incident to arrest only if the arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance or if evidence of the crime of arrest may be found in the vehicle.
Search Incident to Arrest — Vehicle.application: Because evidence of the crime could be found in the vehicle, the search was valid.

Automobile Exception.rule: Police may search a vehicle without a warrant if they have probable cause to believe it contains evidence of a crime.
Automobile Exception.application: Because officers had probable cause, the vehicle search was valid.

Automobile Exception — Scope.rule: Under the automobile exception, police may search any part of the vehicle and containers that may conceal the object of the search.
Automobile Exception — Scope.application: Because the container could hold the object, it was searchable.

Plain View Doctrine.rule: Police may seize evidence without a warrant if they are lawfully present, the item is in plain view, and its incriminating nature is immediately apparent.
Plain View Doctrine.application: Because the evidence was in plain view and obviously incriminating, seizure was valid.

Exigent Circumstances.rule: Police may conduct a warrantless search when exigent circumstances make it impracticable to obtain a warrant.
Exigent Circumstances.application: Because the situation was urgent, the warrantless search was justified.

Exigent Circumstances — Examples.rule: Exigent circumstances include hot pursuit, risk of destruction of evidence, and emergency aid situations.
Exigent Circumstances — Examples.application: Because officers were in hot pursuit, the search was justified.

Stop and Frisk — Terry Stop.rule: Police may briefly detain a person if they have reasonable suspicion of criminal activity.
Stop and Frisk — Terry Stop.application: Because officers had reasonable suspicion, the stop was lawful.

Stop and Frisk — Frisk.rule: Police may frisk a detainee if they reasonably suspect the person is armed and dangerous.
Stop and Frisk — Frisk.application: Because officers suspected the defendant was armed, the frisk was lawful.

Inventory Search.rule: Police may conduct a warrantless inventory search of lawfully impounded property pursuant to standardized procedures.
Inventory Search.application: Because officers followed procedures, the inventory search was valid.

Special Needs Searches.rule: Warrantless searches may be conducted without probable cause when special needs beyond law enforcement make the search reasonable.
Special Needs Searches.application: Because the search served a special need, it was reasonable.

Exclusionary Rule — General Rule.rule: The exclusionary rule prohibits the use of evidence obtained in violation of the Fourth Amendment in the prosecution’s case-in-chief.
Exclusionary Rule — General Rule.application: Because the evidence was obtained unlawfully, it must be excluded.

Exclusionary Rule — Scope.rule: The exclusionary rule applies only to criminal prosecutions and only to government misconduct.
Exclusionary Rule — Scope.application: Because the case is criminal and involved government action, the rule applies.

Fruit of the Poisonous Tree.rule: The fruit of the poisonous tree doctrine excludes evidence derived from an initial illegal search or seizure.
Fruit of the Poisonous Tree.application: Because the evidence was derived from an illegal search, it is excluded.

Independent Source Doctrine.rule: Evidence is admissible if it was obtained from a source independent of the illegal conduct.
Independent Source Doctrine.application: Because the evidence came from an independent source, it is admissible.

Inevitable Discovery Doctrine.rule: Evidence obtained illegally is admissible if it would have been inevitably discovered through lawful means.
Inevitable Discovery Doctrine.application: Because the evidence would have been discovered anyway, it is admissible.

Attenuation Doctrine.rule: Evidence is admissible if the connection between the illegal conduct and the discovery of evidence is sufficiently attenuated.
Attenuation Doctrine.application: Because intervening circumstances weakened the connection, the evidence is admissible.

Good Faith Exception.rule: The exclusionary rule does not apply when police act in objectively reasonable reliance on a warrant later found invalid.
Good Faith Exception.application: Because officers relied in good faith, exclusion is inappropriate.

Good Faith — Limits.rule: The good faith exception does not apply when police rely on a warrant that is facially deficient, issued by a biased magistrate, or based on false information.
Good Faith — Limits.application: Because the warrant was facially deficient, the exception does not apply.

Standing — Personal Rights.rule: A defendant may invoke the exclusionary rule only if the defendant’s own Fourth Amendment rights were violated.
Standing — Personal Rights.application: Because the defendant’s rights were violated, standing exists.

Standing — No Vicarious Standing.rule: A defendant may not challenge a search or seizure based solely on the violation of another person’s rights.
Standing — No Vicarious Standing.application: Because the rights violated belonged to someone else, the defendant lacks standing.

Use of Illegally Obtained Evidence — Imp.rule: Illegally obtained evidence may be used to impeach the defendant’s testimony but not to impeach other witnesses.
Use of Illegally Obtained Evidence — Imp.application: Because the defendant testified inconsistently, the evidence may be used for impeachment.

Harmless Error.rule: A conviction will not be reversed if the admission of illegally obtained evidence was harmless beyond a reasonable doubt.
Harmless Error.application: Because the error did not affect the verdict, the conviction stands.

Fifth Amendment — Self-Incrimination.rule: The Fifth Amendment protects individuals from being compelled to incriminate themselves.
Fifth Amendment — Self-Incrimination.application: Because the defendant was compelled to incriminate himself, the Fifth Amendment applies.

Miranda — General Rule.rule: Miranda warnings are required before custodial interrogation by law enforcement.
Miranda — General Rule.application: Because the defendant was interrogated while in custody, Miranda warnings were required.

Custody — Definition.rule: Custody exists when a reasonable person would not feel free to terminate the interrogation and leave.
Custody — Definition.application: Because a reasonable person would not feel free to leave, the defendant was in custody.

Interrogation — Definition.rule: Interrogation includes express questioning or its functional equivalent likely to elicit an incriminating response.
Interrogation — Definition.application: Because police conduct was likely to elicit a response, interrogation occurred.

Miranda Warnings — Content.rule: Miranda warnings must inform the suspect of the right to remain silent, that statements may be used against the suspect, and the right to an attorney.
Miranda Warnings — Content.application: Because all required warnings were given, Miranda was satisfied.

Miranda — Waiver.rule: A Miranda waiver must be knowing, voluntary, and intelligent.
Miranda — Waiver.application: Because the waiver was knowing and voluntary, statements are admissible.

Invocation of Right to Silence.rule: If a suspect invokes the right to remain silent, interrogation must cease.
Invocation of Right to Silence.application: Because the defendant invoked silence, questioning had to stop.

Invocation of Right to Counsel.rule: If a suspect invokes the right to counsel, all interrogation must cease until counsel is provided.
Invocation of Right to Counsel.application: Because counsel was requested, further interrogation was prohibited.

Reinitiation by Suspect.rule: After invoking Miranda rights, interrogation may resume only if the suspect initiates further communication.
Reinitiation by Suspect.application: Because the suspect reinitiated contact, questioning could resume.

Miranda — Public Safety Exception.rule: Miranda warnings are not required when police questioning is reasonably prompted by an immediate concern for public safety.
Miranda — Public Safety Exception.application: Because officers faced an immediate danger, Miranda warnings were not required.

Use of Statements — Case-in-Chief.rule: Statements obtained in violation of Miranda are inadmissible in the prosecution’s case-in-chief.
Use of Statements — Case-in-Chief.application: Because Miranda was violated, the statement is excluded.

Use of Statements — Impeachment.rule: Statements obtained in violation of Miranda may be used to impeach the defendant’s testimony if voluntary.
Use of Statements — Impeachment.application: Because the statement was voluntary, it may be used for impeachment.

Confessions — Voluntariness.rule: A confession is inadmissible if it is involuntary under the totality of the circumstances.
Confessions — Voluntariness.application: Because coercive tactics were used, the confession is inadmissible.

Voluntariness — Police Coercion.rule: Police coercion is required for a confession to be deemed involuntary.
Voluntariness — Police Coercion.application: Because police used coercive tactics, the confession is involuntary.

Fruit of an Involuntary Confession.rule: Evidence derived from an involuntary confession is inadmissible as fruit of the poisonous tree.
Fruit of an Involuntary Confession.application: Because the confession was involuntary, derivative evidence is excluded.

Sixth Amendment — Right to Counsel.rule: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to counsel in all criminal prosecutions after the initiation of adversarial judicial proceedings.
Sixth Amendment — Right to Counsel.application: Because formal charges had been filed, the Sixth Amendment right attached.

Attachment of the Right to Counsel.rule: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel attaches at the initiation of formal judicial proceedings, such as indictment, arraignment, or filing of charges.
Attachment of the Right to Counsel.application: Because the defendant was indicted, the right to counsel attached.

Critical Stage.rule: Once the right to counsel attaches, it applies to all critical stages of the prosecution.
Critical Stage.application: Because the lineup was a critical stage, counsel was required.

Critical Stage — Examples.rule: Critical stages include post-indictment interrogations, lineups, preliminary hearings, and plea negotiations.
Critical Stage — Examples.application: Because the interrogation occurred after indictment, it was a critical stage.

Offense-Specific Nature.rule: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel is offense-specific and applies only to the charged offense.
Offense-Specific Nature.application: Because questioning concerned an uncharged offense, the Sixth Amendment did not apply.

Waiver of Right to Counsel.rule: A defendant may waive the Sixth Amendment right to counsel if the waiver is knowing, voluntary, and intelligent.
Waiver of Right to Counsel.application: Because the defendant knowingly waived counsel, questioning was permitted.

Police-Initiated Interrogation After Att.rule: After the right to counsel attaches, police may not deliberately elicit statements from the defendant without counsel present.
Police-Initiated Interrogation After Att.application: Because police deliberately elicited statements without counsel, the Sixth Amendment was violated.

Use of Statements — Sixth Amendment Viol.rule: Statements obtained in violation of the Sixth Amendment right to counsel are inadmissible in the prosecution’s case-in-chief.
Use of Statements — Sixth Amendment Viol.application: Because the statement was obtained without counsel, it must be excluded.

Use of Statements. — Impeachment.rule: Statements obtained in violation of the Sixth Amendment may be used to impeach the defendant’s testimony if voluntary.
Use of Statements. — Impeachment.application: Because the statement was voluntary, it may be used for impeachment.

Massiah Doctrine.rule: Under the Massiah doctrine, the government violates the Sixth Amendment by deliberately eliciting statements from a charged defendant through an informant.
Massiah Doctrine.application: Because an informant elicited statements after indictment, the Sixth Amendment was violated.

Pretrial Identification — General Rule.rule: Pretrial identification procedures must comply with the Sixth Amendment right to counsel and the Due Process Clause.
Pretrial Identification — General Rule.application: Because the identification occurred before trial, constitutional protections apply.

Right to Counsel — Attachment.rule: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel applies to pretrial identifications conducted after the initiation of formal judicial proceedings.
Right to Counsel — Attachment.application: Because the lineup occurred after indictment, counsel was required.

Lineups — Right to Counsel.rule: A defendant has the right to counsel at post-indictment lineups and showups.
Lineups — Right to Counsel.application: Because the lineup was post-indictment, counsel was required.

Photographic Identifications.rule: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel does not apply to photographic identifications.
Photographic Identifications.application: Because the identification was photographic, no counsel was required.

Due Process — Suggestive Identification.rule: An identification violates due process if the procedure was unnecessarily suggestive and created a substantial likelihood of misidentification.
Due Process — Suggestive Identification.application: Because the procedure was suggestive and unreliable, due process was violated.

Reliability Factors.rule: Identification reliability is evaluated based on the witness’s opportunity to view, degree of attention, accuracy of prior description, level of certainty, and time between crime and identification.
Reliability Factors.application: Because reliability factors support accuracy, the identification is admissible.

Remedy — Exclusion.rule: An identification obtained in violation of the right to counsel or due process is excluded from evidence.
Remedy — Exclusion.application: Because constitutional rights were violated, the identification is excluded.

In-Court Identification.rule: An in-court identification is admissible if it has an independent source separate from any unconstitutional pretrial identification.
In-Court Identification.application: Because the witness had an independent basis, the in-court identification is admissible.

Right to Jury Trial — Sixth Amendment.rule: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to a jury trial in criminal prosecutions for serious offenses.
Right to Jury Trial — Sixth Amendment.application: Because the offense was serious, the defendant was entitled to a jury trial.

Serious vs Petty Offenses.rule: An offense is considered serious if the authorized punishment exceeds six months of imprisonment.
Serious vs Petty Offenses.application: Because the potential sentence exceeded six months, the offense was serious.

Number of Jurors.rule: A jury must consist of at least six jurors, but need not consist of twelve jurors.
Number of Jurors.application: Because the jury included six jurors, the requirement was satisfied.

Unanimity.rule: A criminal jury verdict must be unanimous.
Unanimity.application: Because the verdict was unanimous, it was valid.

Impartial Jury.rule: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to an impartial jury.
Impartial Jury.application: Because jurors were unbiased, the right was satisfied.

Jury Pool — Fair Cross-Section.rule: Jury pools must represent a fair cross-section of the community.
Jury Pool — Fair Cross-Section.application: Because the jury pool was representative, the requirement was met.

Peremptory Challenges — Equal Protection.rule: Peremptory challenges may not be used to exclude jurors based on race or gender.
Peremptory Challenges — Equal Protection.application: Because jurors were excluded based on race, the challenge was unconstitutional.

Batson Challenge.rule: A Batson challenge requires a prima facie showing of discrimination, a race-neutral explanation, and a determination of purposeful discrimination.
Batson Challenge.application: Because the explanation was pretextual, the Batson challenge succeeded.

Right to Public Trial.rule: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to a public trial.
Right to Public Trial.application: Because the courtroom was improperly closed, the right was violated.

Speedy Trial — Right.rule: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to a speedy trial.
Speedy Trial — Right.application: Because the delay was excessive, the right may have been violated.

Speedy Trial — Barker v. Wingo Factors.rule: Speedy trial claims are evaluated by balancing length of delay, reason for delay, defendant’s assertion of the right, and prejudice to the defendant.
Speedy Trial — Barker v. Wingo Factors.application: Because the delay caused prejudice, a speedy trial violation exists.

Right to Confront Witnesses.rule: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to confront and cross-examine adverse witnesses.
Right to Confront Witnesses.application: Because the defendant was denied cross-examination, the right was violated.

Prosecutorial Misconduct.rule: Prosecutorial misconduct violates due process when it renders the trial fundamentally unfair.
Prosecutorial Misconduct.application: Because the misconduct affected fairness, reversal may be required.

Harmless Error — Trial Rights.rule: A constitutional trial error does not require reversal if it was harmless beyond a reasonable doubt.
Harmless Error — Trial Rights.application: Because the error did not affect the verdict, reversal is not required.

Double Jeopardy — General Rule.rule: The Double Jeopardy Clause prohibits multiple prosecutions or multiple punishments for the same offense.
Double Jeopardy — General Rule.application: Because the defendant is being prosecuted twice for the same offense, double jeopardy applies.

Attachment of Jeopardy — Jury Trial.rule: In a jury trial, jeopardy attaches when the jury is sworn.
Attachment of Jeopardy — Jury Trial.application: Because the jury had been sworn, jeopardy attached.

Attachment of Jeopardy — Bench Trial.rule: In a bench trial, jeopardy attaches when the first witness is sworn.
Attachment of Jeopardy — Bench Trial.application: Because the first witness was sworn, jeopardy attached.

Same Offense — Blockburger Test.rule: Two offenses are the same offense for double jeopardy purposes unless each offense requires proof of a fact the other does not.
Same Offense — Blockburger Test.application: Because each offense requires a different element, double jeopardy does not bar prosecution.

Multiple Punishments.rule: Double jeopardy prohibits multiple punishments for the same offense unless the legislature clearly intended cumulative punishment.
Multiple Punishments.application: Because the legislature intended multiple punishments, double jeopardy is not violated.

Lesser Included Offenses.rule: Double jeopardy bars prosecution for a lesser included offense after conviction or acquittal of the greater offense.
Lesser Included Offenses.application: Because the lesser offense is included in the greater, prosecution is barred.

Acquittal — Finality.rule: An acquittal bars retrial even if the acquittal was based on an erroneous legal ruling.
Acquittal — Finality.application: Because the defendant was acquitted, retrial is prohibited.

Mistrial — Defendant Request.rule: When a defendant requests a mistrial, retrial is generally permitted unless the mistrial was caused by prosecutorial misconduct intended to provoke the request.
Mistrial — Defendant Request.application: Because the mistrial was requested by the defendant, retrial is permitted.

Mistrial — Manifest Necessity.rule: When a mistrial is declared due to manifest necessity, retrial is not barred by double jeopardy.
Mistrial — Manifest Necessity.application: Because manifest necessity existed, retrial is allowed.

Appeals by Prosecution.rule: The prosecution may appeal a dismissal or legal ruling as long as reversal would not require a retrial after acquittal.
Appeals by Prosecution.application: Because reversal would not require retrial, the appeal is permitted.

Separate Sovereigns Doctrine.rule: Double jeopardy does not bar successive prosecutions by separate sovereigns for the same conduct.
Separate Sovereigns Doctrine.application: Because different sovereigns are involved, prosecution is allowed.
